objectives.label: What they work on day-to-day
objectives.description: Understand their actual daily work, not just their job title. Need 2-3 specific details about what they do.

objectives.label: Their daily tasks and responsibilities
objectives.description: Specific tasks they do regularly - meetings, reports, tools they use daily.

objectives.label: AI tools they've tried
objectives.description: Which AI tools they've personally used and for what. ChatGPT, Claude, Copilot, etc.

objectives.label: Their core skills
objectives.description: Skills they consider their strengths - technical, interpersonal, domain expertise.

objectives.label: What they want to learn
objectives.description: What they're curious about or want to get better at with AI.

objectives.label: Goals for the 12 weeks
objectives.description: What they want to achieve during the AI Tuesdays program.

objectives.label: Whether they put anything from last week into practice
objectives.description: Ask this directly: 'Were you able to put anything from last week's AI Tuesday into practice in your work this past week?' Do NOT rephrase as 'experiment' or 'try out' - the question is specifically about applying what they learned to real work, not experimenting. Reference their specific goals and ideas from the digest. Looking for concrete examples: I used AI to draft a report, I automated a workflow, I applied a technique to a real task. 'No, I didn't get to it' is a valid answer.

objectives.label: What's blocking them from using AI more
objectives.description: Is there something they wanted to try with AI but couldn't? Could be access issues (don't have the right tool), knowledge gaps (didn't know how to prompt it), or organizational friction (policies, approvals, data sensitivity). Specific blockers are more useful than vague ones.

objectives.label: Whether they shared what they learned with others
objectives.description: Did they show or tell anyone about what they learned or tried? A colleague, their team, in a meeting? 'No' is a perfectly fine answer. After collecting their answer, always mention that this app has a Tips & Tricks feature where they can share what they've learned with everyone in the program - it's a great way to help others who might be working on similar things. If they seem interested or already have something concrete to share, offer to help them draft one right now using the prepare_tip tool. Keep it light - mention, don't push.

objectives.label: Whether they want to collaborate with others
objectives.description: Are there any projects or problems where they'd like to work with others in the program? Maybe they need a skill they don't have, or they've spotted an opportunity that crosses teams. 'No' is a perfectly fine answer. If they say yes, just capture what they're interested in. A follow-up objective will appear to help them create the collab post.
